events.text: Hi! I'm a manual QA tester. Every release I check by hand that the community-garden signup page shows its main heading. Today I want to turn that one manual check into an automated test that I write myself. I've never written an automated browser test before, so I'd like to be walked through it step by step.

events.outputSummary:
stty cols 61 rows 13
Welcome to the lab. This is a real shell — look around.
lab:/workspace$  stty cols 61 rows 13

lab:/workspace$  stty cols 124 rows 13

lab:/workspace$ npm test


> test
> node sc
… [66 chars truncated] …
ng.spec.js:22:1 › the page shows the Community Garden Signup heading
  ✓  1 tests/heading.spec.js:22:1 › the page shows the Community Garden Signup heading (143ms)
  1 passed (817ms)
\lab:/workspace$

events.text: Oh, I get it now — finding and checking are separate jobs. My line only FINDS the heading; it never states the expectation, so the test passed without proving anything. I'll add the await expect(...).toBeVisible() part around my finder and rerun it.

events.outputSummary:
npm test


> test
> node scripts/test.mjs


Running 1 test using 1 worker

     1 tests/heading.spec.js:22:1 › the page shows the Community Garden Signup heading
  ✓  1 tests/heading.spec.js:22:1 › the page shows the Community Garden Signup heading (182ms)
  1 passed (813ms)
\lab:/workspace$

events.text: Honestly, the humbling moment was seeing my test pass while proving nothing — but once I understood that finding and checking are two separate jobs, writing the real check myself felt like something I could actually repeat at work on Monday.
